schools.summary: Gill: this chapter consists chiefly of commendations and salutations, beginning with the former; it was usual to give letters of commendation for a member of one church to another, and Phebe (by the subscription of the epistle) was the bearer of this letter; Paul commending Phebe, a sister and a 'servant' (diakonos) of the church at Cenchrea (the port of Corinth) — the question whether this denotes a formal office (a deaconess, ministering to the women, the sick, the poor) or generally a faithful servant; the honour given to a faithful woman who served the church, the prominence of women in the labour of the early church (Phebe, Priscilla, Mary, and others).

debates.question: Is Phebe 'a servant (diakonos) of the church' a formal deaconess or a general servant?

sides.position: A formal office (deaconess), ministering to the women, sick, and poor
Generally a faithful servant who ministered to the church

themes: I commend unto you Phebe our sister
a servant of the church which is at Cenchrea
the honour given to a faithful woman

schools.summary: Gill: Paul recommends that they receive Phebe in a kind and friendly manner — into their houses, their hearts' affections, their meetings and fellowship — as one in the Lord and for his sake; receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints, and assist her in whatsoever business she has need, for she hath been a succourer (helper, patroness) of many and of Paul himself — the hospitable reception and practical help due a fellow-saint (the welcome befitting holy people, with love and honour), grounded in her own faithful succour of others (the kindness returned to the kind, the mutual care of the body), Phebe a patroness/benefactor of the church.

themes: receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints
she hath been a succourer of many

schools.summary: Gill: Priscilla (the wife) is sometimes named before her husband Aquila (as here), sometimes after — for, as the Jews say, there is neither first nor last in Scripture, strict order not always observed; Paul greeting Priscilla and Aquila, his helpers (fellow-workers) in Christ Jesus — the honoured couple who laboured with Paul (tentmakers with whom he lodged at Corinth, Acts 18:2-3, who instructed Apollos more perfectly at Ephesus, Acts 18:26); the partnership of husband and wife in the service of Christ, the wife often named first (perhaps for her prominence, zeal, or gifts), the honour to the fellow-labourers.

themes: the partnership of husband and wife in service

schools.summary: Gill: 'laid down their necks' means they exposed themselves to great danger to save the apostle's life (the allusion to execution by beheading, one offering his neck for another) — not literally done but they risked their own lives for him; Priscilla and Aquila risking their lives for Paul's, and not only he but all the Gentile churches giving them thanks — the costly devotion of the couple (who hazarded their own lives), and the gratitude of the whole Gentile church (for in saving Paul they served all the churches he laboured for, the service multiplied), the example of devotion that ventures all for the gospel.

themes: who have for my life laid down their own necks
not only I give thanks, but all the churches

schools.summary: Chrysostom rebukes those who hasten over this part as superfluous (as they do the genealogy in the Gospel), thinking a catalogue of names yields no good — but the gold-founders are careful even of the little fragments, and there is gold here too, in the honour to these saints; Paul greeting the church in Priscilla and Aquila's house (the household congregation meeting in a believer's home, the early house-churches before church buildings) and Epaenetus, the firstfruits of Achaia to Christ (the first convert of the region, the earnest and pledge of the harvest, dear to the apostle) — the honour to the saints in the list, the value of these names.

themes: the church that is in their house
the honour to the first convert and the saints

schools.summary: Gill: some copies read 'on you,' and it seems likely the persons on whom this good woman bestowed so much labour, and to whom she was serviceable, were the saints at Rome where she lived, rather than the apostle and his companions; Paul greeting Mary, who bestowed much labour on the church — the honour to a woman who laboured much in the service of the saints (the diligent, abundant labour of a faithful sister, recorded and honoured by the apostle), the prominence of women's labour in the early church (Mary, like Phebe and Priscilla).

themes: who bestowed much labour on us
the honour to a faithful sister

schools.summary: Gill: Andronicus and Junia were Paul's kinsmen according to the flesh (perhaps of the same tribe and family), fellow-prisoners (having suffered imprisonment for the gospel), of note among the apostles, and in Christ before him; the honoured pair distinguished in the apostolic circle and early in the faith — the debates noted: whether 'Junia' was a woman (the more natural reading of the name, favoured by the early church) or a man ('Junias,' a contracted form), and whether 'of note among the apostles' means they were themselves apostles (in the wider sense) or were well-known to and esteemed by the apostles.

debates.question: Was Junia a woman, and were they apostles?

sides.position: Junia a woman (the natural form of the name, favoured by the early church), and 'of note among the apostles' meaning notable apostles in the wider sense
Junias a man (contracted form), and/or 'of note among the apostles' meaning well-known to and esteemed by the apostles

themes: Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen, and my fellow-prisoners

schools.summary: Gill: a Roman name (Ampliatus in some copies); the apostle had a singular affection for him, and that not on any external account (natural relation, riches, or honour) but in the Lord; Paul greeting Amplias, his beloved in the Lord — the spiritual affection of the apostle grounded not in worldly ties but in their common union with Christ ('in the Lord' both the sphere and the reason of the love, the union the bond, the love because of Christ), the affection for perhaps an obscure believer (the name common among slaves/freedmen) — the love that regards worth in Christ regardless of station.

themes: the spiritual love of the saints

schools.summary: Gill: Urbane a Roman name, called 'our helper in Christ' (a fellow-labourer, some conjecturing a pastor of the church at Rome); Paul greeting Urbane, his (and the church's) helper in Christ ('our helper' the common fellow-worker, not Paul's only), and Stachys, his beloved (the dear fellow-believer) — the honour to those who help in the labour of the gospel and the affection for the beloved saints (the labour, Urbane the helper, and the love, Stachys the beloved, of the body, the service and the affection).

themes: Salute Urbane, our helper in Christ
the affection for the beloved saint

schools.summary: Gill: Origen wondered whether Apelles was the same as Apollos, but had he been intended Paul would have said more (Apollos so eminent); the character 'approved in Christ' very considerable; Paul greeting Apelles, approved in Christ (tried, tested, and found genuine in the faith, perhaps through persecution or temptation, 1 Peter 1:7, 'the trial of your faith'), and the believers of Aristobulus' household (his servants or family members who believed) — the honour to a tried and approved believer (the proved, genuine faith) and the notice of the saints in a household (the gospel reaching the family/servants).

themes: them which are of Aristobulus' household
the honour to a tried believer

schools.summary: Gill: Herodion was Paul's kinsman (of the same nation, tribe, or family); Clarke: probably another relative of the apostle; Paul greeting Herodion, his kinsman (both a relative by blood and a fellow-believer, the natural kinship crowned by the spiritual), and those of Narcissus' household 'which are in the Lord' — the careful qualification (only those of the household who were believers, not the whole household, some of whom may not have believed) — the gospel reaching households in part (some members believing, some perhaps not), the honour to the kindred and household saints.

themes: the household of Narcissus, which are in the Lord

schools.summary: Gill: Tryphena and Tryphosa were women (said to be noble women of Iconium whom the apostle converted), their names Greek; Paul greeting Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord, and the beloved Persis, who laboured much in the Lord — the honour to faithful women who laboured in the Lord's work (the present labour of two and the abundant past labour of another, the labour 'in the Lord' for his cause and service), the degrees of labour (the present labour and the 'much' labour), the prominence of women's labour in the early church (like Phebe, Priscilla, and Mary).

themes: Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord
the beloved Persis, which laboured much in the Lord

schools.summary: Gill: Rufus a Roman name; some have thought this the same Rufus who was a son of Simon of Cyrene (who carried Christ's cross, Mark 15:21), which is not unlikely; Paul greeting Rufus, 'chosen in the Lord' (either chosen/elect generally, or choice, eminent, distinguished in the Lord, a select believer), and his mother whom Paul calls 'mine' also — the honour to a choice believer and the tender notice of the motherly woman who had been a mother to Paul (in kindness, care, and love, the spiritual motherhood), the possible touching link to Simon of Cyrene's family becoming believers.

debates.question: Does 'chosen in the Lord' mean elect or choice/eminent?

sides.position: Either chosen/elect in the Lord, or choice, eminent, distinguished in the Lord (a select, excellent believer)

themes: Salute Rufus chosen in the Lord
the motherly woman who cared for Paul

schools.summary: Gill: the first two names are Greek; these persons, with Herodion before, are noticed in the Roman martyrology as disciples of the apostle (Asyncritus said to be bishop of Hyrcania, Phlegon of Marathon, Hermas of Philippi, of the seventy disciples); Paul greeting Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermas, Patrobas, Hermes, and the brethren with them — the salutation of a group of believers (perhaps a household or a congregation that met together) and the brethren associated with them — the honour to the saints (named and grouped), the notice even of the unnamed brethren, the fellowship of the saints gathered together.

themes: the brethren which are with them
the honour to the saints named and grouped

schools.summary: Gill: Philologus a Greek name (a man's, meaning a lover of learning), Julia a woman's name, Roman, probably his wife; Paul greeting Philologus, Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the saints with them — the salutation of another group of believers (perhaps families, with husband and wife, brother and sister) and all the saints associated with them — the honour to the named saints and the comprehensive greeting to all the saints gathered with them (the named and the unnamed, the whole company, the comprehensiveness of Paul's love embracing all the saints).

themes: Philologus, and Julia, Nereus, and his sister, and Olympas
all the saints which are with them
the honour to the named saints

schools.summary: Gill: Christian salutation a wishing all temporal, spiritual, and eternal happiness to one another, hearty and sincere; the 'holy kiss' alludes to the custom of friends kissing at meeting or parting, in token of love and affection; Paul exhorting the saints to salute one another with a holy kiss (pure, reverent, chaste, the kiss of charity, 1 Peter 5:14, the token of holy, sincere love within the congregation) and conveying the greeting of the churches of Christ — the love among the saints and the mutual communion of the churches (the fellowship between congregations, the one body of Christ).

themes: salute one another with an holy kiss
the churches of Christ salute you

schools.summary: Chrysostom: how gently Paul exhorts — not in the character of a counsellor but that of a servant, with much respect, calling them brethren and supplicating them ('I beseech you, brethren'); Paul beseeching the brethren to mark (note, observe, be on guard against) those who cause divisions (factions) and offences (stumbling-blocks) contrary to the doctrine they had learned, and to avoid (shun, turn from) them — the warning against false teachers and troublers who divide the church contrary to the apostolic doctrine (identified by their contrary teaching and dividing effect), the protection of the church from the dividers.

themes: mark them which cause divisions and offences
contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned
the protection of the church from dividers

schools.summary: Gill: such serve not Christ — they preach not him but themselves, seek not his honour but their own things (worldly honour, riches, grandeur), and seek to please men; the dividers serve not Christ but their own belly (their appetites, gain, self-interest, under the guise of religion, the belly the master), and by good words and fair speeches (plausible, smooth, flattering, eloquent) deceive the hearts of the simple (the unwary, undiscerning) — the self-serving motive and the deceptive method of the false teachers (the marks of the deceiver: the self-interest and the smooth words).

themes: serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly
by good words and fair speeches deceive the simple

schools.summary: Gill: the fame of their obedience to the gospel was spread everywhere, commended among all the churches; Paul mentions this as a reason to beware of false teachers, lest they reproach their reputation; Paul rejoicing in the Romans' renowned obedience, yet desiring them to be wise unto that which is good (discerning, skilled in the good) and simple concerning evil (innocent, harmless, unskilled in it, kept pure, Matthew 10:16, 'wise as serpents, harmless as doves') — the wise and innocent saint, the call to wisdom connecting to the warning against the dividers (the wise saint discerns the false teacher, the innocent is not beguiled).

themes: your obedience is come abroad unto all men
wise unto that which is good

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the God of peace' (who promotes peace) shall 'bruise' (subdue, gain the victory over) Satan; the language refers to Genesis 3:15; it denotes Paul's confidence that they would overcome the arts of those sowing discord; Paul promising that the God of peace will shortly bruise Satan under their feet (the assured victory over Satan, the author of the divisions, v.17-18, echoing the first promise/protevangelium of Genesis 3:15, the serpent's head crushed by the seed of the woman, fulfilled in the saints), and pronouncing the grace of the Lord Jesus (the benediction closing the warning, the comfort and the blessing).

themes: the God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly
the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you

schools.summary: Gill: now follow the salutations of the apostle's friends and companions; Timotheus (Timothy) named first — the disciple Paul met at Derbe (Acts 16:1), whose mother was a Jewess and father a Greek; Paul conveying the greetings of his companions — Timothy his fellow-worker (beloved son in the faith, constant companion), and Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater his kinsmen (relatives or fellow-Jews) — the salutations of the faithful companions who were with Paul as he wrote, sending their love to the Roman saints (the fellowship of the workers, the picture of the apostle surrounded by his company).

themes: Lucius, and Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen

schools.summary: Gill: Tertius a Latin name; he was certainly an amanuensis (a scribe), who wrote the epistle as Paul dictated it; Tertius, who wrote (penned) the epistle, sending his own greeting in the Lord — the notice of the scribe who took down the apostle's words, allowed to add his own salutation; the honour to the faithful amanuensis (the humble helper included, named in the inspired record), and the glimpse of how the epistle was written (Paul dictating, Tertius writing, as Paul often used an amanuensis, adding the closing in his own hand), the greeting grounded 'in the Lord.'

themes: I Tertius, who wrote this epistle
the honour to the faithful scribe
the glimpse of how the epistle was written

schools.summary: Gill: Gaius (likely the one Paul baptized at Corinth, 1 Corinthians 1:14) was the apostle's host and of the whole church (his hospitality and generosity well attested, some thinking him the Gaius to whom John wrote his third epistle); Paul conveying the greetings of Gaius his host (and of the whole church, his home open to the apostle and congregation), Erastus the chamberlain/treasurer of the city (a man of civil office among the believers), and Quartus 'a brother' (no office, just a brother in Christ) — the company of saints of various stations (host, official, humble brother), all one in Christ.

themes: Gaius mine host, and of the whole church
Erastus the chamberlain of the city
saints of various stations, all one in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this verse placed by the Syriac at the end of the chapter, and wanting in some copies, being (except 'all') the same as v.20; the reason for the repetition may be that the former was written by his amanuensis and this with his own hand, as was Paul's custom in all his epistles (the closing benediction in his own hand as a token of genuineness, 2 Thessalonians 3:17); Paul repeating the benediction — the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with all the saints (the apostolic blessing of grace, the sum of all blessing, upon all without exception), the Amen sealing it.

themes: the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all

schools.summary: Gill: God described by his power, the particular instance the establishing of his people (in the gospel and the profession of it, with grace in their hearts and in its exercise); Matthew Henry: Paul solemnly closes his epistle with a magnificent ascription of glory to God; to him who is able to establish the saints according to the gospel and the preaching of Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery kept secret since the world began — the establishing power of God by the gospel (the saints confirmed and settled in the word), and the gospel as the revelation of the mystery (the hidden plan of salvation, especially the inclusion of the Gentiles, long hidden but now made known).

themes: him that is of power to stablish you
the revelation of the mystery, which was kept secret
the establishing power of God by the gospel

schools.summary: Gill: the mystery now made manifest in these last days, in which God has spoken by his Son, by whom the doctrines of grace are most clearly revealed; Barnes: now revealed, made known to all nations by the writings of the prophets and the gospel; the mystery, once hidden, now made manifest — by the scriptures of the prophets (intimated and foretold in the Old Testament, now fully revealed), according to the commandment of the everlasting God (by his order and authority, the eternal God whose hidden plan is now revealed in time), made known to all nations (the Gentiles included, the universal proclamation) for the obedience of faith (that all might believe and submit to the gospel).

themes: by the scriptures of the prophets

schools.summary: Gill: this may be understood of God the Father, the only wise God — originally, essentially, and infinitely wise (not excluding the Son and Spirit), whose wisdom appears in creation, providence, redemption, and the whole scheme of the gospel; the glory of all to be given to him through Christ as Mediator; Paul closing the epistle with the ascription of glory to God only wise, through Jesus Christ, for ever — the doxology crowning the epistle (the glory ascribed to the all-wise God whose manifold wisdom, Ephesians 3:10, the whole epistle has displayed in the plan of salvation), the glory rendered through Christ the Mediator (all access and praise through him), world without end, the Amen sealing it.

themes: to God only wise, be glory
the wisdom of God in salvation
